content.assessment: The Finnish reform lens offers little support for a passive baseline. Finland's experience shows that whole-system reform to address rising identification and regional strain was undertaken deliberately in 2010-2011 rather than left to drift; inaction in Hungary would forgo the opportunity to pre-empt the kind of unmanaged category growth Finland's reform was designed to address. However, Finland's later erosion of comprehensive-school equity (post-2000, contested) shows that even reformed systems face drift pressures from school choice and segregation, so a 'do nothing' baseline is not unique in being subject to erosion — the question is whether inaction versus reform changes the trajectory's speed or direction.
opportunities: Could be paired with reconciled measurement (as in Finland's decomposition of reclassification versus true prevalence change) to determine whether Hungary's drift resembles the intensified-support reclassification pattern
strengths: Provides a clear counterfactual against which any of Finland's reform mechanisms could be benchmarked for comparative evaluation
threats: Even under active reform, Finland shows persistent regional variation (4%-13%) and post-2000 equity erosion linked to school choice, so passive drift in Hungary risks compounding both identification strain and choice-driven segregation simultaneously, with no reform lever to counteract either
weaknesses: Finland's proactive 2010-2011 restructuring in response to rising identification demonstrates that comparable systemic drift was judged to require active redesign rather than passive observation
Passive baseline does not engage with any tracking-age, comprehensive-provision, or political-durability lessons the Finnish case offers
